displayTitle: Why Tiny Cracks Break Big Parts

beats.narration: Why does a crack the size of a hair matter on a part the size of a truck axle? It's worth knowing cold.

payload.title: HAIR VS. AXLE
payload.sub: A crack the width of a hair. A part the size of an axle.

beats.narration: Stress flows like water. A round hole squeezes it to about three times average; a sharp crack crowds it far higher.

payload.heading: Stress squeezes around a flaw
payload.note: ROUND HOLE ≈ 3× · SHARP CRACK FAR HIGHER

beats.narration: Material and duty set the stakes: a ductile bracket blunts the crack, a hardened pin can fracture, a cyclic shaft keeps it growing.
A fatigue crack starts at a surface stress raiser, grows a step each load cycle, then races to fracture. Your window is early.

payload.heading: How fatigue cracks grow
payload.note: GROWTH ACCELERATES LATE — THE WINDOW IS EARLY

beats.narration: MT hunts small, sharp, surface flaws — exactly where failures start. So find it small, report it honestly, and let the criteria decide.

payload.sub: The criteria decide what lives and what dies.
